Client 2: And I’m Emily. I’m mostly looking for something simple we can stick with, honestly.
Advisor: That makes sense—we’ll keep it plain-English. To start, what’s the household gross income each year, before taxes?
Client 1: Household gross income is 139950 a year.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to keep me straight—William, you’re Client 1, and Emily, you’re Client 2, correct?
Client 1: Yes—William is Client 1.

Client 2: I do—mine is smaller. My gross income is 50100 a year.
Advisor: Got it. Shifting to spending—what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in?
Client 2: We’re pretty steady—total monthly expenses are 3300.
Advisor: And do you have any monthly debt payments at all—mortgage, car loans, credit cards?
Client 1: No, monthly debt payments total is 0.

Client 2: And I’m Emily. I’m mostly looking for something simple we can stick with, honestly.

Client 1: Yes—William is Client 1.

Client 2: None. No mortgage, no car payment—nothing like that.
Advisor: Okay. Let’s move to assets—investments plus the home. What total investable assets number do you track?
Client 1: Yes—investable assets total is 889950.
Advisor: Great. Let’s break that down. William, do you have retirement accounts like a 401(k) or IRA, and what’s the value?
Client 1: Yes, my 401(k) and IRA bucket is 300700.

Client 1: Yes, my 401(k) and IRA bucket is 300700.
Advisor: And for the home, what value are you using for your primary residence?
Client 1: Primary residence value is 263500.
Advisor: Emily, you have a taxable account held through an insurance company—what’s the value there?
Client 2: Yes, that’s mine—358850.

Advisor: That makes sense—we’ll keep it plain-English. To start, what’s the household gross income each year, before taxes?
Client 1: Household gross income is 139950 a year.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to keep me straight—William, you’re Client 1, and Emily, you’re Client 2, correct?
Client 1: Yes—William is Client 1.

Client 2: Simple is good.
Advisor: Simple is good—agreed. Let’s grab a couple household basics so I don’t make assumptions. William and Emily, what state do you consider your primary residence for planning and taxes?
Client 1: We’re in FL.
Advisor: Got it. And household size—just confirming, it’s the two of you, right? How many adults in the household?
Client 2: Two adults—2.

Advisor: Hi William, hi Emily—nice to meet you both. Before we dive into details, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m William. We’re both 69 and retired, and I just want to make sure we’re not missing anything—health costs, market swings, that sort of thing.
Client 2: And I’m Emily. I’m mostly looking for something simple we can stick with, honestly.
Advisor: That makes sense—we’ll keep it plain-English. To start, what’s the household gross income each year, before taxes?
Client 1: Household gross income is 139950 a year.

Advisor: Perfect. And marital status—are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, married.
Advisor: Thanks. And then, just so I’m clear on how you want to work together—are you looking for an ongoing relationship where we manage and advise as a registered investment advisor, or more of a one-time plan?
Client 2: Ongoing is fine. The channel would be RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Okay—thank you. Emily, when you say ongoing, is that because you want fewer moving parts to track?

Advisor: Got it. And household size—just confirming, it’s the two of you, right? How many adults in the household?
Client 2: Two adults—2.
Advisor: Perfect. And marital status—are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, married.
Advisor: Thanks. And then, just so I’m clear on how you want to work together—are you looking for an ongoing relationship where we manage and advise as a registered investment advisor, or more of a one-time plan?

Advisor: Got it. And dependents—anyone financially dependent on you two right now? Kids you still support, grandkids, anyone like that?
Client 2: No—0 dependents.
Advisor: Okay. Next one is more of a planning input: how would you describe your overall risk tolerance—conservative, moderate, aggressive—what fits best?
Client 1: Moderate.
Advisor: Moderate—thank you. Emily, does that feel right to you too, or would you say a notch more conservative?

Client 2: And I’m Emily. I’m mostly looking for something simple we can stick with, honestly.
Advisor: That makes sense—we’ll keep it plain-English. To start, what’s the household gross income each year, before taxes?
Client 1: Household gross income is 139950 a year.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to keep me straight—William, you’re Client 1, and Emily, you’re Client 2, correct?
Client 1: Yes—William is Client 1.

Client 2: I’m probably a little more conservative day-to-day, but overall, yeah, moderate is fair.
Advisor: That’s helpful. Now taxes—do you know roughly what bracket you tend to land in these days? Even a band is fine.
Client 2: I think it’s 35%.
Advisor: Okay—35%. I’ll stick with that unless your CPA tells you otherwise. Let’s shift into income like we said—monthly streams. William, you mentioned rental income—what’s the annual amount you want us to use?
Client 1: Rental income is 11650 annually.

Advisor: Hi William, hi Emily—nice to meet you both. Before we dive into details, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m William. We’re both 69 and retired, and I just want to make sure we’re not missing anything—health costs, market swings, that sort of thing.
Client 2: And I’m Emily. I’m mostly looking for something simple we can stick with, honestly.
Advisor: That makes sense—we’ll keep it plain-English. To start, what’s the household gross income each year, before taxes?

Advisor: Perfect—thank you. That gives me the full picture across income, assets, liabilities, and protection.
Advisor: Okay—thanks, William, thanks, Emily. Before we wrap this section, I want to tie the income back to each of you as individuals. William, what gross annual income number should I associate with you personally?
Client 1: For me, gross annual income is 89850.
Advisor: Got it. And just to confirm your status—are you fully retired at this point, William, or any part-time work still happening?
Client 1: I’m retired.

Advisor: Great. Let’s break that down. William, do you have retirement accounts like a 401(k) or IRA, and what’s the value?
Client 1: Yes, my 401(k) and IRA bucket is 300700.
Advisor: And for the home, what value are you using for your primary residence?
Client 1: Primary residence value is 263500.
Advisor: Emily, you have a taxable account held through an insurance company—what’s the value there?

Client 1: Yes, joint bank account—81550.
Advisor: Thanks. On protection—any insurance policies we should include, like life, long-term care, or disability?
Client 1: I still have a disability policy.
Advisor: Okay—do you know the monthly premium and the coverage amount?

Advisor: Hi William, hi Emily—nice to meet you both. Before we dive into details, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m William. We’re both 69 and retired, and I just want to make sure we’re not missing anything—health costs, market swings, that sort of thing.
Client 2: And I’m Emily. I’m mostly looking for something simple we can stick with, honestly.
Advisor: That makes sense—we’ll keep it plain-English. To start, what’s the household gross income each year, before taxes?
